title: Chapter Two: The Unwanted Son
content:
> *"The Jeong Family Divine Sword: three sword intents, twenty-one techniques, derived from the first half of the Southern Edge Sect's Thirty-Six Heavenly Swords. A generous description would call it 'adapted.' An honest one would call it 'cobbled together by a man with half a manual and too much ambition.'"*
> -- Internal assessment, Southern Edge Sect archives, author unnamed (likely an elder who'd had enough)

---

Fifty-seven.

Fifty-eight.

Fifty-nine.

The wooden pillar across my shoulders weighs roughly as much as a grown man, and my thighs are screaming -- not the good kind of burn that means progress, but the deep, grinding kind that means *you've been doing this for two hours, you absolute lunatic, and if you don't stop soon your knees will file a formal complaint.*

Sixty.

I drop into a horse stance at the bottom of the squat, hold it until my legs shake, then drive back up. The muscles across my back flex and contract in a way that would probably alarm a physician. My body doesn't look like it belongs to a fifteen-year-old. It doesn't look like it belongs to a *human,* if I'm being honest -- the internal energy I've been circulating for years has compressed my muscles into something dense and hard, like the grain of ironwood. Diamond-cut, almost alien.

My body is a masterpiece. It's also a ticking bomb. But we'll get to that.

Sometimes, at the top of a squat, when the blood is pounding in my temples and the wood is biting into my shoulders and my vision narrows to a single bright point -- sometimes I feel something *shift* at the crown of my head. Not pain, exactly. More like pressure. Like a door being pushed from the other side. The first time it happened, I was twelve, and I sat down so fast I almost cracked my tailbone. Now it's just -- a thing that happens. The cost of having a body that processes energy the way mine does.

A cicada screams in the elm tree above the east wall. The sound drills into my temples, sharp enough to make me wince.

I don't tell anyone about it. Who would I tell?

*Sixty-seven. Sixty-eight. Sixty-nine.*

My vision goes white for half a second. Just a flash -- like sunlight reflected off water, blinding and brief -- and then the world comes back, sharper than before, every detail etched in crystal clarity.

*That's new.*

I file it away in the part of my brain where I keep all the things my body does that it shouldn't, and I keep squatting.

Seventy.

"Young master, it's time for the banquet!"

The servant's voice carries across the training ground -- thin, reedy, carefully positioned at the maximum possible distance from me. He's standing at the edge of the gravel path, weight shifted onto his back foot like he's ready to bolt. None of the servants come close if they can help it. The boy who killed his mother just by being born, the third son nobody wanted -- I make people uncomfortable simply by existing.

*I should finish up.*

Ten more reps. I lower the wooden pillar onto the rack -- the wood groans against the brackets -- and grab my discarded shirt from the fence post. The fabric is damp from the humidity, sticking to my skin as I pull it on over what amounts to a body built for war in a family that wishes I'd never been born.

The Jeong Family Dynamic Technique -- *my* technique, the one I created by ripping apart the family's useless meditation manual and rebuilding it as a moving cultivation method that strengthens the body while moving instead of while sitting cross-legged like a decorative statue -- has reached its peak. I can feel it the way you feel a wall when you press your palm flat against stone. There's nowhere higher to go. My body can't get any stronger than this.

I should be proud of that. I *am* proud of it, in the quiet, private way you're proud of something when there's nobody around to share it with. A year of obsessive training, a completely original martial art, a body that would make Shaolin monks sit down and take notes.

And what good is any of it when the gate in your skull is trying to kill you?

*Don't think about that. Not now.*

Unless I'm just a frog in a well, staring up at the Henan sky and calling it the universe.

Which -- honestly? Probably.

"Young master!"

"I'm coming."

I say it the way I say everything to the servants: flat, unbothered, like a boy who doesn't care about anything except his next meal. It's easier that way. For them and for me.

The banquet is for my second brother, Jeong Jung-san -- the one with the angular jaw and the desperate need to be praised. He's been accepted as a disciple of the Southern Edge Sect, one of the Nine Great Sects, which in our tiny corner of Xinye County is roughly equivalent to being selected for godhood. Father's been planning this dinner for weeks. You'd think Jung-san had already become a Taoist immortal, the way the family's been carrying on.

I pass the guest quarters on my way to the hall and catch the sound of guest disciples reciting from the Great Ming Code -- the foundational text of the empire, the one that tells the story of how the Elves came down from the gates of heaven and helped Taizu destroy the Yuan dynasty and establish the Ming.

"The compatriots with long ears and small stature returned from the gates of heaven, destroyed the evil Yuan, and established the great Ming with Taizu."

Familiar words. I grew up on them. Every child in the Central Plains did. The Elves -- the *compatriots* -- are woven into our history like golden thread, revered and strange and untouchable.

An Elf told my father I'd die before twenty.

I smile thinly and keep walking.

The banquet hall hits me with a wall of noise and the smell of properly fried chicken -- which, I'll admit, almost makes the whole ordeal of being a Jeong worth it. Almost.

"You're late, foolish one."

My eldest half-brother, Jeong Nam-san, greets me with narrow eyes and a sneer that he probably practiced in front of a mirror. My chest muscles twitch -- not from intimidation, but because my body does weird things when I'm suppressing the urge to say something I'll regret.

"It's been a while, brother."

"If you're late, hurry up and take a seat."

I nod and head to the far end of the table. My seat -- always the lowest, below even my younger siblings, because the family head's indifference rolls downhill and the first and second wives kick anyone who's already lying on the ground. I'm a direct descendant in name only. A ghost with an appetite.

The first wife -- Nam-san's mother -- gives me a look as I pass that could curdle milk. I keep my eyes forward and slide onto the bench. The wood is smooth from years of use, worn to a dull shine by everyone who's sat here before me. The second wife doesn't even bother looking. Somehow, that's worse.

*It was convenient because of that.*

That's what I tell myself. That's the story I've constructed -- the third son who turned his family's contempt into freedom, who used their neglect as shelter for his training, who doesn't care because caring would be a waste of energy he can't afford to spend. No obligations. No responsibilities. Just meals and training, training and meals. The life of a well-fed ghost.

A convenient story. Almost true enough to believe.

I pick up my chopsticks and reach for the fried chicken. The oil glistens under the lantern light, golden-brown and crisp, and the smell -- garlic and scallion and the particular char of iron-wok cooking -- almost makes me forget where I'm sitting.

*Almost.*

The thing about telling yourself you don't care is that you have to keep telling yourself. Every time a sibling looks through you. Every time your father's gaze passes over the seat where you're sitting like the chair is empty.

Across the table, Jung-san laughs at something Father says, leaning forward with his angular jaw propped on one hand. The gesture is easy, natural -- the posture of a son who has never once questioned whether he belongs at this table. I bite into the chicken and chew slowly.

You build the wall higher. You make the joke sharper. You train harder, eat better, sleep faster, and you do not -- under any circumstances -- let the wall crack. Because what's behind it isn't strength or indifference or the cool detachment of a martial genius who's above petty family politics.

What's behind it is a little boy who went to his brothers with something he'd made -- something brilliant, something that could have helped all of them -- and was laughed out of the room.

*They must be overjoyed.*

Father's gaze is locked on Jung-san, warm in a way it has never been when aimed at me. Not once. Not even when I was small enough to still believe he might come around.

I fill my bowl with rice and eat.

The conversation flows around me like a river around a stone -- politics of the major sects, the Southern Edge Sect's rising star nicknamed the Cloud Sword Dragon, speculation about whether Mount Hua can still compete. Jung-san is already calling the Cloud Sword Dragon "senior brother," which is either optimistic or delusional, depending on how charitable you're feeling.

I reach for a rice cake.

And stop.

Father is staring at me. Not warmly. Not even neutrally. The look is the one he uses when I've done something to remind him I exist.

"I hear the third spends all day in the training ground." Father sets down his wine cup with a deliberate *clack.* "How is your progress with the Jeong clan's Divine Sword?"

Every head at the table turns. The sudden quiet is deafening -- even the servants carrying platters freeze mid-step. I set down my chopsticks and sit up straight, folding my hands in my lap where no one can see my knuckles whiten.

"The principles of our family's martial arts are difficult for me to grasp, so it's hard to make much progress. I'm ashamed."

This is a lie. The Jeong Family Divine Sword is -- and I mean this with all the respect I can muster, which is approximately none -- a mess. Three sword intents, twenty-one techniques, all of it cobbled together from the first half of a manual my grandfather half-memorized during his time as a secular disciple of the Southern Edge Sect. The sword paths don't make sense. The transitions are clunky. It's like watching someone try to play a concerto on an instrument they built from spare parts.

I could fix it. I *wanted* to fix it, once, years ago -- wanted to fix it and share it and be useful to this family that doesn't want me. I went around to my half-brothers, bright-eyed and stupid, recommending they try the dynamic cultivation method I'd created.

"You created martial arts?"

"What if you go mad from practicing it? Will you take responsibility if someone dies?"

"Do you think you have the qualities of a great master?"

The mockery was sincere. That was the worst part -- they weren't being cruel for sport. They genuinely couldn't fathom that the unwanted third son might have done something extraordinary. Their world didn't have room for that possibility.

So I stopped trying to share. And something in my chest -- something small and hopeful and embarrassingly young -- went quiet.

I pick a piece of gristle from between my teeth and flick it onto the plate. The lanterns flicker overhead as a servant opens the side door, letting in a gust of night air that smells like jasmine and horse manure from the stables.

*A great master, huh.*

In the martial world, a great master means the founder of a style worthy of establishing a sect. Zhang Sanfeng of the Mysterious Sect. Bodhidharma of Shaolin. Names that echo through centuries. I've never thought of myself in those terms. I just wanted everyone to be healthy together.

*That's not how the story went, though, is it?*

"The first and second sons are already approaching mastery," Father says, his voice carrying the weight of judgment. He lifts his wine cup, takes a measured sip, sets it down without looking at me. "Though your efforts are commendable and I won't ask further, you should reflect on whether you are truly putting in effort in the training ground."

"I will heed your advice."

I clasp my hands, bow, and wait until his gaze moves on. Nam-san smirks into his rice bowl. A younger sibling to my left shifts away from me on the bench -- just an inch, barely perceptible, as if whatever I have might be contagious.

*Showing interest will only hurt me.*

I turn back to the chicken. The skin crunches between my teeth, still hot enough to burn the roof of my mouth. I let it.

Across the table, my fifth older sister catches my eye and quickly looks away. She's twelve -- young enough that the mothers' poison hasn't fully set in yet, old enough that she knows it's coming. For a second, something flickers across her face that might be sympathy.

I give her the smallest possible nod. She doesn't nod back.

*See? This is why you don't care. Because the ones who might care don't dare, and the ones who dare don't care.*

I eat another piece of chicken and let the conversation wash over me like rain on stone.

And then the world shifts.

"Southern Edge masters have arrived!"

The gatekeeper's shout cuts through the banquet like a sword through silk. Every head whips toward the door. Father, who was sitting with the ponderous gravity of a man who considers himself very important, scrambles to his feet so fast he nearly overturns his cup.

"Quickly, quickly welcome them! No, I'll go myself!"

I should leave. I should slip away to the training ground and pretend none of this concerns me, because it *doesn't* concern me -- I'm not the one going to Mount Zhongnan, I'm the one staying here to squat with a log on my shoulders until my body finally gives out or my skull pops open, whichever comes first.

But this is a major sect. Real martial artists. Masters from the mountain where Taoism was born.

*When else would I get to see this?*

I follow the crowd to the main gate.

Father is already there, bowing so deeply his forehead nearly touches the ground, clasping his hands to three figures standing in the torchlight. The family members behind him -- brothers, sisters, wives, stewards, servants -- are doing the same, some of them prostrating entirely.

And at the front of the three visitors stands someone who makes my breath catch.

Golden hair like sunlight poured into silk. Ears like long, tapered leaves. Features that are beautiful the way a blade is beautiful -- precise, otherworldly, and slightly dangerous.

An Elf.

"An Elf...!" my eldest brother groans behind me, staring with his mouth half-open. *Subtle, brother. Really subtle.*

"I am deeply grateful for your warm welcome," the Elf says, and her smile is a thing that makes your eyes feel cleaner for having witnessed it. "I am Yeo Il-shin, an elder of the Southern Edge Sect."

An elder.

Of a major sect.

Which means she's not just any Elf -- she's a founding contributor to the empire. An ageless being who stood alongside the sect's first ancestor, who helped drive out the Yuan dynasty, who has walked this earth for centuries while humans around her grew old and crumbled to dust.

"I... I failed to recognize an immortal!"

Father's voice cracks. People prostrate. The whole courtyard drops to its knees like a puppet show where someone's cut all the strings at once.

*Isn't that going too far?*

I bow -- moderately, with clasped hands, because respect is one thing but smashing your face into the ground is another -- and lift my head just slightly.

Her eyes are on me.

Not on Father. Not on Jung-san, the chosen disciple. Not on the prostrating masses.

On *me.*

Something in those ancient, leaf-green eyes goes still. Curious. Like she's seen something she didn't expect to find in a place like this.

My stomach drops -- not fear, exactly, but the cold, electric jolt of being *seen* by someone who knows what they're looking at. The torchlight catches the gold in her hair, and for a moment the courtyard narrows to just the two of us -- her ancient gaze and my fifteen-year-old body standing among the prostrated crowd like a nail that refuses to lie flat.

I lower my head. My fingers curl against my thighs, nails pressing half-moons into my palms.

An Elf elder of a major sect looked at me. At *me.*

At the ghost.

A breeze moves through the courtyard, carrying the smell of torch oil and crushed gravel and the faint sweetness of the river beyond the walls. I swallow hard against the heat building behind my eyes -- not tears, but something worse. The terrifying, dangerous feeling of being *noticed.*

*Don't,* I tell myself. *Don't you dare make this into something it isn't.*

I turn around and head for the training ground. My sandals crunch against the gravel path, too fast, and I force myself to slow down. The night air cools the sweat on the back of my neck.

Nobody's watching.

Nobody ever is.
